              About
            
            
              
                
            
          
        
        
        
          
            I am currently a PhD student studying community ecology
            at Stanford University working with Dr. Fiorenza Micheli at
            the Hopkins Marine Station. My main research interest is
            considering how communities are formed, maintained and
            disassembled. I’m especially interested in how these
            dynamics vary over time and comparing across different
            ecosystems/contexts. 
            PhD Biology Stanford University, Current
            MSc Biology University of Victoria, 2017
            BScH Biology Queen’s University, 2013
            Email: jmcirwin@stanford.edu
            Twitter: @jamiemcirwinGoogle
            Scholar

articles.title: Outreach
articles.contents:
Linked with both my research and teaching experiences, I’m committed
to furthering diversity, equity and inclusion within academia. I helped
co-create and run the Stanford
Biology PhD Preview Program, which helps prospective students
nagivate the application process and leverage their strengths within
their applications. I also worked on the DIMES
Network (Diversifying and Integrating Marine Education at Stations)
where we created an ecology field curriculum for undergraduate students
along with existing virutal field trips to increase access to coastal
field stations.
In addition, I have written about my research for various blog posts
(HMS
High Tidings, ICRS
Reefbites Feature Friday) and have interacted with the public on
World Ocean’s Day as a scientific diver and at the Hopkins Marine
Station running the surfgrass touch tank.


Showing students from the Middlebury Institute of International
Studies my field experiment in Big Sur, California; Photo Credit: Meghan
McKenna

articles.title: Research
articles.contents:
Ecosystems around the world are experiencing escalating threats
including both the loss of consumers and changing disturbance patterns.
These changes often act jointly to affect ecosystems: after
disturbances, ecosystems undergo succession and consumers can play an
integral role in structuring these developing communities.
For my PhD research, I’m interested in how consumers affect
communities as they assemble and recover from disturbances. I’m
asking the questions:
How do large fishes influence coral reef benthic succession and
coral recruitment?

Experiment in the Chagos Archipelago, Photo Credit: Kristina
Tietjen
How do large fishes influence community variability across space
and turnover through time during coral reef succession? How do large
fishes influence coral recruitment through grazing and grazing
scars? 
Experiment in Palmyra Atoll, Photo Credit: Joe Wible
How does herbivore arrival timing influence rocky intertidal
succession? 
Experiment in Big Sur, California

Previously, I conducted a variety of research ranging from
conservation policies of marine fishes in Canada (McDevitt-Irwin et
al. 2015), nursery role of seagrass ecosystems (McDevitt-Irwin et
al. 2016), to coral microbiome community ecology (McDevitt-Irwin et
al. 2017, McDevitt-Irwin et al. 2019).

articles.title: Teaching
articles.contents:
I have worked as a teaching assistant at the University of Victoria,
the Bamfield Marine Sciences Centre, and Stanford University. Some of my
best experiences have been mentoring undergraduate students, through
programs like CSUMB REU Program, MITACS,
Stanford BSURP, or mentoring honors thesis students at Stanford. In
addition, I have created and taught coding workflows in RMarkdown and
GitHub for both local high school students and other graduate
students.
I am committed to learning and growing as an instructor and have
taken various courses on course development and how to be an inclusive
and effective teacher to diverse students (e.g. Inclusive
and Effective Teaching, Science and
Engineering Course Design). In addition I shadowed a professor at
West Valley Community College through the Preparing
Future Professors program.

Fieldwork with my interns at Big Sur, California; Photo Credit: Sarah
Pierce
